nameMeanings.name: Oldhall
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: locative name from Oldhall Farm in Rock, Oldnall Farm in Old Swinford (both Worcs), or from a lost place called Oldnall End in Berkswell (Warwicks), which is recorded as Oldenhale in 1327. The place-names derive from the Old English personal name Ealda or Old English ald ‘old’ + Old English halh ‘nook, corner of land’.

variants.name: Olner
meanings: English: perhaps a variant of Oldnall , with dissimilation of l-l to l-r.

variants.name: Oldale
meanings: 1 English: locative name, probably from Wooldale (WR Yorks), which is recorded as Wulvedale in 1307. The place-name derives from Old English wulf ‘wolf’ + Old English dæl or Old Scandinavian dalr ‘valley’. The local pronunciation of the place-name, with loss of initial w-, is given as [u:dl] in Place-Names of WR Yorks .

2 English: locative name, occasionally from one or other of the many places in England named Old Hall, such as Old Hall in Rayne (Essex), which is recorded as Old Halle in 1425–35, and probably derives from Middle English olde ‘old’ + halle ‘large residence, hall’ (Old English ald, hall).
3  English: perhaps a variant of   Oldnall  .

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.
